title: Introducing a bought mated queen
subtitle: How to safely introduce a new queen using a candy-plug introduction cage.
excerpt: Requeening with a bought mated queen is faster than raising your own and lets you introduce known genetics. The colony will usually reject a foreign queen placed directly on the frames. A slow release through a candy-plug cage gives the bees time to accept her pheromone before she is free.
sourceNotes: National Bee Unit, Queen Introduction Guide (2024). BBKA Technical Bulletin on queen introduction. Ted Hooper, Guide to Bees and Honey (1976), queen introduction chapter. British Standard queen-introduction cage specifications, BIBBA guidance notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A soft sugar paste that blocks one end of a queen introduction cage. The workers eat through it from outside while the queen eats from inside. The process takes two to four days and allows the colony to gradually accept the queen's pheromone before she is released.

The window between removing the old queen and introducing the new one. A colony made queenless for 24 hours is more receptive to a new queen. More than two to three days queenless may trigger the colony to start raising emergency queen cells from worker eggs, which complicates introduction.

An aggressive response in which workers form a tight cluster around a queen they are rejecting. The queen overheats and can die within minutes. Balling is more likely when the introduction is rushed, when the colony has virgin queens present, or when a strange scent is on the queen.

The group of eight to twelve workers that attends the queen at any moment, feeding and grooming her and spreading her pheromone through the colony by contact. Observing a calm retinue around the queen is a good sign she has been accepted.

content.text: A bought mated queen arrives in a small wooden or plastic cage with a
candy plug
at one end and two or three attendant workers. Handle the cage gently. Do not expose it to cold, direct sun, or vibration. Introduce her within 24 hours of arrival.
Prepare the colony first
The colony must be queenless before you introduce a new queen. Remove the old queen at least 24 hours before introduction. This is the
queenless period

. Do not leave the colony queenless for more than two days before introduction. A colony that has started raising emergency cells is less receptive and those cells must all be found and removed before you introduce the new queen.
Check every frame for queen cells during this preparation inspection. Remove every one you find, including any small cups with eggs. A single missed queen cell is enough to get your new queen killed.
Preparing the introduction cage

Remove the travelling cap from the candy-plug end of the cage. Do not remove the cork or foam from the candy end yet. Check that the candy is intact and not dried out. If the candy plug looks cracked or hard, add a small amount of soft baker's fondant to the end of the cage with a matchstick before placing it in the hive.

attrs.title: Remove the attendant workers
attrs.body: Some beekeepers remove the two or three attendant workers from the cage before introduction. The attendants were from the breeder's colony and carry a foreign pheromone signature. Removing them reduces the chance of balling. Tip them gently onto a frame. The new queen will be picked up by the resident workers through the cage mesh and will not be alone.

content.text: Placing the cage

Push the cage gently between two central brood frames so the mesh faces downwards. The bees must be able to reach the candy and make contact with the queen through the mesh. Do not press the cage so tight that bees cannot get around it. Close the hive and leave it undisturbed for 48 to 3 days.
The release check

After 48 hours, open the hive calmly. Check whether the queen has been released by the workers eating through the candy. If she is out, look for her on the surrounding frames. A released queen with a calm
retinue
of attendant bees has been accepted. Close the hive and wait seven days before inspecting again.

If the candy has not been eaten through after 3 days, gently release the queen manually by removing the candy end. Lower her onto a brood frame and watch for five minutes. If workers gather round her calmly, she is accepted. If they form a tight ball, this is
balling
. Spray a small mist of plain water over the cluster to disperse it. Do not try to pull the ball apart with your fingers.
Confirming acceptance

Seven days after introduction, inspect for eggs in the brood frames. A freshly laid egg stands upright in the base of a cell. If you see eggs, the new queen is laying and the introduction has worked. If you see no eggs but the colony is calm and there are no queen cells, wait another seven days. A queen that has not yet mated or has not started laying after two weeks should be replaced.

entries.cue: Workers are clustered tightly on the cage mesh after 24 hours
entries.cause: Colony is still defensive; a virgin queen or laying worker may be present
entries.fix: Open the hive and check every frame for queen cells or a virgin queen. Remove any found. Wait another 24 hours before checking cage behaviour again.

entries.cue: New queen is dead inside the cage after release
entries.cause: Workers chewed through the cage mesh or balled her through it before full acceptance
entries.fix: Leave the colony queenless for another 24 hours, remove every queen cell again, and try a fresh introduction cage with a new queen. Some colonies are chronically difficult to requeen and need a full shook swarm reset.

entries.cue: No eggs after two weeks but no queen cells either
entries.cause: Queen may not have mated successfully; weather during mating period was poor
entries.fix: Wait one more week. If still no eggs, the queen is a drone layer or has failed. Remove her and start a new introduction with a fresh queen.
